summary: Gruber reviews the iPhone 16e after six days of use, framing it as the spiritual successor to early Steve Jobs-era iPhones in its simplicity and restraint. He highlights the single-lens camera as both the phone's defining aesthetic trait and its key tradeoff, noting it makes the phone dramatically thinner and lighter than the iPhone 16 and 16 Pro. He methodically addresses the missing features — MagSafe, ProMotion, ultra wideband — arguing that Apple's target audience for this phone neither knows nor cares about any of them. On pricing, he defends the $600 starting price as a logical rung in Apple's pricing ladder and dismisses complaints that it should be cheaper while also having more features. The 16e is positioned as an iPhone for people who want an iPhone but don't want to think about their phone.
key_points: The single-lens camera makes the 16e dramatically thinner and lighter than the iPhone 16 and 16 Pro, with no mesa protrusion and only 1.7mm of lens protrusion — a throwback to early iPhones.
Missing features like MagSafe, ProMotion, ultra wideband, and Wi-Fi 7 are irrelevant to the target buyer, who charges via cable and has no idea what Wi-Fi version numbers mean.
The 16e camera trades image quality (especially in low light) for simplicity, but its shorter minimum focus distance (12cm vs 24cm) partially compensates for lacking macro mode.
The $600 price fits neatly into Apple's $100-increment pricing matrix, and expecting a cheaper price while demanding more features misunderstands Apple's market positioning.
Apple's first custom C1 modem performed without complaint, with cellular connections that were fast and strong throughout testing.
money_quotes: The iPhone 16e looks like a phone with a tiny camera on the back. The iPhone 16 Pro looks like a camera that also happens to be a phone.

If you know what an ƒ-stop is, you probably shouldn't buy an iPhone 16e. If you don't know what an ƒ-stop is, you probably won't notice any difference in camera quality from an iPhone 16 or even 16 Pro.
It's like bragging about the refresh rate on the dashboard display in a Kia Sorento.
Complaining that Apple no longer makes a phone that's priced in the $400-500 range is like complaining that BMW no longer makes any cars that cost less than $40,000.
The iPhone 16e is an iPhone for people who don't want to think much about their phone. But they do want an iPhone, not just any 'whatever' phone.
One of the most surprising aspects of my professional life in recent years is how much time I spend thinking and writing about magnets.
Daring Fireball readers aren't in the 16e demographic; it's the friends and family members of DF readers who are.
key_insight: The iPhone 16e succeeds precisely because of what it omits, delivering the essential iPhone experience — good screen, decent camera, great battery — without the complexity and bulk that power users demand but casual buyers never notice.
